data.source: European Social Survey (ESS) rounds 1–4; additional country-level indicators (e.g., far-right party popularity, social protection expenditure, World Bank Governance Indicators).
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual (with observations nested in country-rounds and countries)
data.notes: Dependent variables measured on 0–10/11-point trust scales, reverse-coded to represent political distrust; construction of a three-item index (range 0–10) with interitem correlations 0.48–0.58 and Cronbach’s alpha 0.76; governance indicators combined via PCA (avg interitem corr 0.80, alpha 0.94). Country-level covariates include far-right vote share/popularity, social protection expenditure per capita, GDP per capita, and governance quality. Some descriptive references to Eurobarometer; one section references use of British Election Studies data and an instrumental variables analysis.

method.description: Three-level multilevel (hierarchical) models estimate the relationship between concern about immigration and political distrust toward national institutions (parliament, politicians, legal system) using ESS 2002–2009. Models include extensive individual- and country-level controls, as well as cross-level interactions (e.g., immigration concern by long-term immigration-country status and by far-right party popularity). Variance components and bivariate correlations are reported to contextualize relationships.
steps: Obtain ESS rounds 1–4 (2002–2009) and harmonize variables across rounds.
Construct dependent variables: trust in parliament, politicians, and legal system; reverse-code to form political distrust scales (0–10/11-point).
Create indices: a three-item index (range 0–10), verify reliability (alpha ~0.76); combine governance indicators via PCA (alpha ~0.94).

Prepare individual-level predictors: concern about immigration; interpersonal trust/distrust; dissatisfaction with economy, health, and education (reverse-coded); unhappiness/life dissatisfaction; frequency of meeting friends; left-right self-placement; voting behavior (far-right, government/opposition; winner/loser); demographics (income, age, education, gender).

Assemble country-level variables: far-right party popularity (vote share), social protection expenditure per capita, GDP per capita, governance quality; code long-term immigration-country dummy (e.g., Germany/UK = 1; Southern Europe = 0).
Specify a three-level HLM with individuals nested in country-rounds nested in countries; estimate models separately for each institution and with pooled specifications.
Estimate bivariate Pearson correlations between immigration concern and distrust outcomes at individual and country levels; compute variance components.
Test cross-level interactions: immigration concern × long-term immigration-country; immigration concern × far-right popularity.
Assess model fit and variance explained at each level; interpret coefficients, interactions, and robustness across specifications.
Optionally, conduct supplementary analyses noted in the study (e.g., instrumental variables) if applicable and data/identification permit.
method.models: Three-level hierarchical linear models (multilevel models); multivariate regressions with cross-level interactions; Pearson correlations; variance components analysis; (one section references instrumental variables analysis).
method.outcome_variable: Political distrust toward national institutions: parliament, politicians, and the legal system (reverse-coded trust scales; higher = more distrust).
method.independent_variables: Primary: concern about immigration. Additional: interpersonal distrust, dissatisfaction with national economy, dissatisfaction with health and education systems, unhappiness/life dissatisfaction, social capital (interpersonal trust; frequency meeting friends), economic perceptions (individual and national), left-right self-placement, voting for far-right party, winner/loser effect, household income, age, education, gender, governance quality, social protection expenditure, GDP per capita, long-term immigration-country status, far-right party popularity.
method.control_variables: Interpersonal trust/distrust; dissatisfaction with economy, health, and education; unhappiness/life dissatisfaction; social capital measures; economic perceptions; left-right ideology; voting behavior (far-right, government/opposition, winner/loser); household income; age; education; gender; governance quality; social protection expenditure; GDP per capita; country and country-round random effects.

results.summary: Concern about immigration is positively and significantly associated with political distrust toward parliament, politicians, and the legal system. The association is stronger in long-term immigration countries and modestly amplified where far-right parties are more popular. Individual-level dissatisfaction with the economy and interpersonal distrust also strongly predict political distrust. Most variance lies at the individual level, but substantial country and country-round variation is explained by the models. Social protection expenditure is associated with lower distrust. The relationship is more pronounced for national institutions than for European ones.
